title: Chapter 6 Quiz
description: Test your mastery of building custom functions!

questions.question: What is the primary reason for using functions in C?
options: To make the code run slower.
To group reusable code blocks together to avoid writing the same code multiple times (DRY principle).
To hide code from the compiler.
To translate C code into C++.
questions.explanation: Functions prevent you from copying and pasting the exact same lines of code all over your project.

questions.question: If a function definition starts with the keyword `void`, what does it signify?
options: The function does not return any value back to the code that called it.
questions.explanation: `void` means empty return type. It executes code (like printing something) but doesn't hand back a calculated answer.

questions.question: Why should you generally place your custom functions ABOVE `main()` in your code file?
options: Because the C compiler reads top-to-bottom and needs to know what the function is before main() tries to use it.
Because functions are heavier than main().
It doesn't matter; the compiler searches the whole file first automatically.
questions.explanation: C expects a function to be defined (or prototyped) before it is called. Otherwise, it throws an 'undeclared identifier' warning/error.

questions.question: What is a 'Parameter' in the context of functions?
options: A setting in the C compiler.
An empty variable defined inside the function's parentheses `()` waiting to receive data when called.
The curly braces {} surrounding the function.
questions.explanation: Parameters are the variables defined to accept input data, like `int width` in `calculateArea(int width)`.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Parameter and an Argument?
options: Parameters are defined in the function setup; Arguments are the actual values passed in during the function call.
They are strictly the exact same thing.
Arguments are strings, Parameters are ints.
Parameters go in `{}`, Arguments go in `()`.
questions.explanation: Parameter: `(int x)`. Argument: passing the actual number `5` when doing `myFunc(5)`.

questions.question: When defining multiple parameters inside a function, you must separate them using a _____.
questions.explanation: Multiple parameters are separated by commas, like `(int x, int y)`.

questions.question: If you want a function to calculate an equation and give the Integer answer back to the main code, how must you start the function definition?
questions.explanation: You replace `void` with the data type of the expected answer (`int`).

questions.question: What keyword is used inside a function to actually send the answer back out?
questions.explanation: The `return` statement physically hands the data back out of the function.

questions.question: A `return` statement instantly terminates the entire function, skipping any code written below it.
questions.explanation: Yes. The absolute moment `return` executes, the function dies and hands control back to whoever called it.

questions.question: What describes 'Local Scope'?
options: A variable that works across the whole internet.
Variables created inside a function's `{}` braces are invisible and inaccessible to any other function.
Code written on a local computer.
questions.explanation: Functions create a "bubble" (Scope) around their internal variables. Those variables are born when the function starts and completely destroyed when the function ends.

questions.question: If you want a variable to be perfectly visible and modifiable by EVERY single function in your file, where do you declare it?
options: At the bottom of the file
At the top of the file, outside of all curly braces {}
questions.explanation: This creates a 'Global Variable' which every function has full access to.

questions.question: It is considered 'Best Practice' to use Global Variables for absolutely everything to avoid dealing with Scope.
questions.explanation: Global variables are actually discouraged because ANY function can accidentally overwrite or corrupt the data, making tracking down bugs a nightmare.

questions.question: If function `A()` declares `int score = 5;` and function `B()` declares `int score = 10;`, what happens?
options: C crashes with a 'Duplicate variable' error.
The code compiles perfectly because the two 'score' variables live in completely separate Local Scopes and cannot see each other.
Function B steals the variable from Function A.
questions.explanation: Because of Local Scope, the variables are isolated. Function A has its own box called 'score', and Function B has an entirely different box also called 'score'.

questions.question: Can you call a function multiple times?
options: Yes, as many times as you like, from anywhere.
No, functions can only be called once.
Yes, but only if they are void functions.
questions.explanation: That is the entire point! You write it once, and you can call it 10,000 times if needed.

questions.question: What is the very first function that the C compiler looks for to begin executing the program?
questions.explanation: Execution of a standard executable C program always begins strictly inside the `main()` function block.

questions.question: To correctly use `printf` inside a pure math function, that file must have `#_____ <stdio.h>` at the top.
questions.explanation: `#include` adds the Standard I/O library to your code so it knows what `printf` does.

questions.question: If a function evaluates `return;` (with no value) what kind of function MUST it be?
questions.explanation: In a `void` function, you can write `return;` to instantly exit the function early without sending any data.

questions.question: You can pass variables as arguments into a function call, like `calculateArea(x, y);`.
questions.explanation: Yes, C passes copies of the variable's values dynamically into the function.

questions.question: If `main()` calls `int result = add(5, 10);`, what essentially happens under the hood?
options: The program halts and goes inside `add()`, does the calculation, throws `15` back as the answer, and assigns it to 'result'.
It stores the string 'add(5, 10)' into result.
questions.explanation: The function call itself is literally evaluated and swapped out with its return value.

questions.question: If your function takes `(float cost, float tax)`, what happens if you try to pass `("Ten", "Five")` as arguments?
options: The program translates the strings to floats automatically.
The code compiles but runs very slowly.
The C compiler throws a Type Mismatch Error because it strictly expects floats.
questions.explanation: C is statically typed. If a parameter demands a float, you MUST pass it a float. Giving it a String will crash compilation.
